title: Chicken and Ham Pie
subtitle: Tender chicken and cooked ham in a creamy white sauce under golden puff pastry
excerpt: Chicken and ham pie uses two kinds of cooked meat in a well-seasoned white sauce. The ham adds a salty depth that the chicken alone does not have. It is one of the more forgiving pies to make, and it holds well.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: chicken and ham pie entry, white sauce method. Combination documented across British Victorian household manuals as a practical way to use cold cooked meats.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the assembled, unbaked pie for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight in the fridge and bake as normal, adding 10 minutes to the time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to two days ahead and refrigerate. Top with pastry and bake on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white sauce made from a pale roux cooked out in stock rather than milk. It is one of the five French mother sauces and forms the base of many cream pie fillings and soups.

content.text: Chicken and ham pie is the practical cousin of the more glamorous party centrepieces. It uses good chicken thighs and a piece of cooked gammon or leftover ham, folded into a stock-based white sauce and baked under puff pastry. The ham brings salt and flavour the chicken alone cannot provide.
The sauce here is a velouté rather than a bechamel: roux cooked out in chicken stock, then finished with cream. This gives a cleaner flavour that lets the ham and chicken come through.

items.prepNote: cooked and diced into 2 cm pieces, or use leftover cooked ham
to season (taste before adding: the ham is salty)

content.text: Season the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} boneless chicken thigh pieces with pepper only (the ham adds enough salt). Heat a little oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat and brown the chicken in batches for 4 to 5 minutes until golden. Transfer to a plate.
In the same pan, add a little butter and fry the {{onion}} diced onion over a medium heat for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and thyme leaves and stir for 1 minute.
Make the sauce and finish the filling
In a separate saucepan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over a medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes to make a pale roux. This is the base of the

. Gradually whisk in the {{stock-chicken}} of warm chicken stock, then the {{double-cream}} of double cream. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes until thickened. Season carefully with pepper; add salt only after tasting, as the ham is already salty. Stir in the mustard if using.

Combine the chicken, onion mixture, {{gammon}} of diced ham, and sauce in one pan. Simmer gently for 10 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Taste and adjust seasoning. Cool the filling completely before topping.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Spoon the cooled filling into a pie dish. Roll the puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and lay over the top. Press the edges to seal, trim, and crimp. Brush with beaten egg. Cut two small slits in the centre.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Salt was added before tasting, and the gammon provided more than expected.
items.fix: Always taste the finished sauce before seasoning with salt. A good gammon makes the filling salty enough without any addition.

items.cause: Overcooked in the browning stage, then overcooked again in the sauce.
items.fix: Brown the chicken quickly for colour, not to cook through. The 10-minute simmer in the sauce is sufficient to finish it.

items.cause: The sauce was too thick before assembly, and thickened further in the oven.
items.fix: The finished sauce should coat a spoon lightly and flow when tilted. If it holds a firm shape off the heat, add a little more stock.

items.symptom: Pastry is pale after the baking time
items.cause: Oven temperature was lower than expected.
items.fix: Use an oven thermometer to check the actual temperature. Continue baking and check every 5 minutes; the pastry needs to be fully golden, not just set.

content.text: Chicken and ham pie sits firmly in the British tradition of using two cold meats in a cream sauce as a proper dinner rather than an afterthought. the Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton gives a version that uses leftover cooked chicken and ham from a joint, assembled in a dish and topped with pastry. The modern version works just as well with fresh chicken thighs and a piece of gammon cooked the same day. The combination of poultry and cured pork is one of the most reliable in British cooking; the same logic that makes a ploughman's lunch work also makes this pie.
